paper:867767318044737939.authors: Sam Azadi | N.D. Drummond | W.M.C. Foulkes
paper:867767318044737939.en_title: Quasiparticle Effective Mass of the Three-Dimensional Fermi Liquid by Quantum Monte Carlo
paper:867767318044737939.zh_title: 通过量子蒙特卡罗方法计算三维费米液体的准粒子有效质量

variables.content: The quasiparticle effective mass m* is defined from the derivative of the quasiparticle energy band ε(k) at the Fermi wave vector k_F as m* = k_F · (dε/dk)^{-1}_{k_F}, where k_F is the Fermi wave vector and derivatives are evaluated with respect to the magnitude k of the momentum; in atomic units the bare electron mass m equals 1. For the paramagnetic two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) with spin polarization ζ = 0 and density parameter r_s = 1 (where r_s = (1/(πρ))^{1/2} with electron number density ρ), variational Monte Carlo (VMC) and fixed-node diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit produce values consistent with the bare mass within statistical uncertainty: m*_{VMC}(r_s = 1) = 0.98(1) and m*_{DMC}(r_s = 1) = 0.95(1) (atomic units), indicating negligible mass renormalization from electron–electron interactions at this high density.

For the two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) model consisting of N interacting electrons of charge −e and bare mass m in a finite periodic simulation cell of area A (so that the density is ρ = N/A) and with density parameter r_s = (1/(πρ))^{1/2}a_B^{-1} (using atomic units with Bohr radius a_B = 1), the finite-size dependence of the quasiparticle effective mass m*(N) obtained from quantum Monte Carlo band-derivative estimates is taken to follow the empirical form
      m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2}
      for some constant β, and this empirical scaling relation is applied to the authors' simulation data obtained at N = 146, 218, and 302 electrons to extrapolate m*(∞) for densities in the metallic range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5.
      Holzmann et al., Theory of finite size effects for electronic quantum Monte Carlo calculations of liquids and solids, Phys. Rev. B 94, 035126 (2016).

Let N denote the number of electrons in a finite periodic simulation cell used to compute quasiparticle bands by quantum Monte Carlo; for paramagnetic (ζ = 0) 2D-UEL data obtained at N = 146, 218, and 302 electrons and for densities in the metallic range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5, the finite-N dependence of the extracted quasiparticle effective mass m*(N) is empirically well described by m*(N) = m*(∞) + α·N^{-3/2} for some constant α, and extrapolation of the VMC and DMC data using this N^{-3/2} scaling yields thermodynamic-limit estimates m*(∞) that increase monotonically with r_s across the studied points, although the VMC-extrapolated and DMC-extrapolated m*(r_s) trends have different slopes.

Diffusion Monte Carlo (DMC) calculations of the three-dimensional homogeneous electron gas (3D-HEG), extrapolated to the infinite-system-size (thermodynamic) limit, predict that the quasiparticle effective mass m* decreases as the density is reduced (i.e., as the density parameter r_s increases from r_s = 1 to r_s = 10), where r_s = ((3/(4π n))^{1/3} a_B^{-1}) with electron number density n and Bohr radius a_B = 1 a.u.; the Fermi wavevector k_F is the wavevector corresponding to the electron density. The thermodynamic-limit effective mass m* is obtained from a fitted single-particle energy band E(k) by the relation m* = k_F / (dE/dk)_{k_F}, where (dE/dk)_{k_F} denotes the derivative of E(k) with respect to the wavevector magnitude k evaluated at k = k_F. Two spin polarizations were studied: paramagnetic (zero spin polarization, equal populations of up and down spins) and fully ferromagnetic (all electron spins aligned); the decrease of m* with increasing r_s is present for both spin polarizations, and the ferromagnetic m* falls more rapidly with r_s and is smaller than the paramagnetic m* at every density parameter r_s considered (r_s = 1,2,3,4,5,10).

For the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic, ζ = 1) two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) studied in the metallic density range 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5, VMC and fixed-node DMC calculations performed for simulation cells containing N = 151 electrons indicate that the quasiparticle effective mass m* decreases as r_s increases (i.e., m* is suppressed as the density is reduced), a trend opposite to that found for the paramagnetic case over the same density interval.

For the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG over the density-parameter range \\(r_{s}=1\\) to 10, let \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) denote the quasiparticle effective mass extrapolated to the thermodynamic limit by fitting the finite-size DMC effective masses to the single-power-law form \\(m^{*}(N)=m^{*}(\\infty)+b\\,N^{-\\gamma}\\) and adopting the exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) for the extrapolation. The proposition is: the extrapolated numerical values \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\) indicate that the true thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass of the clean continuum-model paramagnetic 2D HEG remains close to unity across this \\(r_{s}\\) range and that there is no significant enhancement of \\(m^{*}\\) at low density for this physical system class.

Extrapolating the finite-size DMC effective-mass data to the thermodynamic limit using the single-power-law scaling with exponent \\(\\gamma=3/2\\) yields the following quasiparticle effective masses \\(m^{*}(\\infty)\\) expressed in Hartree atomic units (a.u.): for the paramagnetic (unpolarized) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.955(2)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=1.04(2)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=1.03(4)\\); for the fully spin-polarized (ferromagnetic) 2D HEG, \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=1)=0.851(5)\\), \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=5)=0.74(1)\\), and \\(m^{*}(r_{s}=10)=0.70(3)\\). These numerical values are the principal thermodynamic-limit benchmarks reported for clean, continuum-model 2D HEGs in this work.

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional uniform electron liquid (2D-UEL) with spin polarization ζ = 0 and for density parameters in the metallic interval 1 ≤ r_s ≤ 5, applying Slater–Jastrow–backflow (SJB) trial wave functions, band fitting (quartic or Padé), and the empirical finite-size extrapolation m*(N) = m*(∞) + β·N^{-3/2} to data at N = 146, 218, and 302 yields extrapolated thermodynamic-limit values m*(∞) that form a monotonic increasing function of r_s across the studied discrete points; this monotonicity statement is conditional on those methodological choices (SJB nodal improvements, fitting form, and N^{-3/2} extrapolation) being adequate for the datasets considered.
      Fig. 7 (this paper)

For the paramagnetic two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the same extrapolation procedure applied to the DMC quartic-fit band data yields the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass m^*(∞)=1.04(2) a.u., with m^* defined as m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F}.

For the fully spin-polarized two-dimensional homogeneous electron gas at density parameter r_s=5, the thermodynamic-limit quasiparticle effective mass obtained by extrapolating finite-N DMC quartic-fit results is m^*(∞)=0.74(1) a.u., using m^*=k_F/(dE/dk)_{k_F} with k_F the ferromagnetic Fermi wave vector.
